skillCheck.passText: Fine, I'll tell you. I've been stalling because the project means a lot to me and I'm afraid that once I start, I'll find out I'm not good enough to finish it. The missing piece wasn't really the problem, my confidence was. You showing up and making things happen shamed me into admitting I was hiding behind excuses.
skillCheck.failText: I'm just careful about getting everything lined up before I commit. That's all.

templates.rewardFlavor: a generous payment and first look at whatever I end up making

templates.expositionText: I owe {target_npc} in {target_npc_settlement} a favor from last season, and I need to make good on it before I lose their trust entirely. They're a {target_npc_role} and they asked me for a simple thing that I've been too busy to deliver. Would you go explain the delay and assure them it's coming?
templates.acceptText: Appreciated. I've been dreading that conversation, and having someone else open it takes the edge off.
templates.declineText: Then I'll write them. Again. A third letter probably won't make things better, but at least It silence.
templates.expositionTurnInText: {target_npc} is still willing to wait? That's more patience than I deserve. Now let me take care of the reason I've been delayed in the first place.
templates.conflict1Text: The {enemy_type_plural} around here have been chewing through my schedule. Every time I set out to finish something, they're in the way. {kill_count} of them cleared and I can finally follow through on every promise I've been making.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: That opens everything up. I can move freely again, and the first thing I'm doing is making good on that favor.
templates.resolutionText: The favor is repaid, {target_npc} is satisfied, and my reputation is intact. Take {reward_item}. You helped me keep a relationship I was about to lose through neglect, and that's worth more than the coin.

skillCheck.passText: The way you put that, I couldn't help but laugh. You're right. I've been treating a simple favor like it was some impossible burden, and the longer I waited, the bigger it grew in my head. Hearing someone describe my situation with that kind of lightness made me realize how absurd I've been about the whole thing.
skillCheck.failText: I just got behind. It happens to everyone eventually.

templates.rewardFlavor: honest coin and a friendship worth more than the amount
templates.targetNpcOpener: The {self_role} in {settlement_name} sent you? I was beginning to think they'd forgotten. I'm not upset, just surprised it took this long. The favor itself was never a big ask.
templates.targetNpcCloser: Tell them we're fine. I'd rather they show up late than not at all.

templates.expositionText: I promised {settlement_npc} a {quest_item} two weeks ago and I still haven't delivered. It's that I kept choosing to do easier things first. If you could find one for me, I'd stop lying awake about it.
templates.acceptText: Good. The guilt has been worse than the errand would have been.
templates.declineText: Then I'll go myself tomorrow. Or the day after. Or whenever I run out of other excuses.
templates.expositionTurnInText: That's the one. I feel lighter already. But before I hand it over, there's something else I should have dealt with while I was procrastinating.
templates.conflict1Text: The {enemy_type_plural} near the south path have been a nuisance for everyone, and I volunteered to organize a response and then never did. {kill_count} of them taken care of would let me stop dodging {settlement_npc}'s questions about that too.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: Done. I can actually make eye contact with the neighbors again. One more thing, and then I'm caught up on everything.
templates.conflict2Text: {target_npc} in {target_npc_settlement} asked me to pass along a message weeks ago, and I forgot until just now. Would you go see them and let them know the message was received, even if the delivery was shamefully late? They're a {target_npc_role} and they won't hold it against you.
templates.conflict2TurnInText: {target_npc} took it well? Good. That's the last thread hanging, and it's finally tied off.
templates.resolutionText: Every promise kept, every favor returned, every loose end dealt with. Take {reward_item}. You did in three days what I spent two weeks avoiding, and the relief alone is worth twice what I'm paying you.

skillCheck.passText: You're right that there's a pattern in how I've been behaving. My grandmother used to say that avoiding small duties invites larger failures, and she meant it as a spiritual warning, not just practical advice. I've been ignoring that teaching and the guilt isn't just about the errand. It's about becoming the kind of person she warned me not to become.
skillCheck.failText: I just got behind on a few things. It a crisis, just a backlog.

templates.rewardFlavor: a fair payment and the relief of a clear conscience
templates.targetNpcOpener: A message from {settlement_name}? I sent that weeks ago and heard nothing back. I assumed it got lost on the road, honestly. Good to know it arrived, even late.
templates.targetNpcCloser: No harm done. Tell them I appreciate the follow-up.

templates.expositionText: I found marks on the trees near {settlement_name} that I can't identify. They're too regular to be animal scratches and too old to be recent. There's a {quest_item} that might help me compare them against known patterns. If you could find one, I'd have something to work with besides staring and guessing.
templates.acceptText: Good. I've been losing sleep trying to make sense of them on my own.
templates.declineText: I'll keep studying them with what I have. Puzzles don't solve themselves, but they don't expire either.
templates.expositionTurnInText: The patterns match up in ways I didn't expect. The marks are territorial, left by something that considers this area claimed. That changes things, because whatever left them is still out there.
templates.conflict1Text: Now that I know what we're dealing with, I need {kill_count} of the {enemy_type_plural} in the area handled. They're the ones leaving the marks, and their claim on this territory is going to become a real problem if it goes unanswered.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: The marks will fade in time, and nothing is leaving fresh ones. Mystery solved and problem handled in the same stroke.
templates.resolutionText: I can walk past those trees now without that nagging feeling in my chest. Take {reward_item}. You helped me find the answer and then deal with what the answer meant, and both parts mattered.

skillCheck.passText: Oh, you've seen marks like these before? Then maybe you'll understand why I'm not sleeping. I saw the same kind near my old home before I came to {settlement_name}, and a season of attacks followed that drove my family out. I've been downplaying the urgency because I don't want to start a panic, but I know what these marks mean.
skillCheck.failText: Interesting theory, but I think they're just old scratches. Nothing to get worked up about.

templates.rewardFlavor: what I can pull together and a story I'll tell properly if you ask

templates.expositionText: The ground near the old well has been humming, not loudly, just enough to feel it through your boots if you stand still. Nobody believes me because it stops when they come to listen. Bring me {gather_count} {quest_item} and I can set up a proper comparison between the well site and normal ground.
templates.acceptText: Thank you. I was starting to think I'd imagined it, and that's worse than any answer would be.
templates.declineText: I'll keep standing there with my boots on, then, waiting for someone to believe me.
templates.expositionTurnInText: The samples confirm it. The soil near the well has a different composition, almost like something changed it from underneath. This isn't my imagination, but I need someone who understands this kind of thing.
templates.conflict1Text: {target_npc} in {target_npc_settlement} is a {target_npc_role} who has dealt with ground disturbances before. Take the samples to them and ask what they make of it. I need an answer I can bring to the council without sounding like I've lost my mind.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: So {target_npc} says it's a mineral deposit shifting under pressure. Natural, nothing sinister, and it'll settle on its own within a season. I'm relieved, embarrassed, and grateful in roughly equal measure.
templates.resolutionText: The humming has already lessened, exactly the way {target_npc} said it would. Take {reward_item}. You helped me prove I wasn't hearing things, and then you helped me learn the things weren't dangerous. Both parts mattered.

skillCheck.passText: You felt it too, standing there just now? I watched your feet shift. That's the part nobody believes because they never stay still long enough. I feel it every night through the floor of my house. I moved my bed to the far wall weeks ago and I haven't told a soul, because once you say you feel the ground humming in your sleep, people stop taking you seriously about anything.
skillCheck.failText: It's just a curiosity. I'd like it explained, that's all.

templates.rewardFlavor: a modest sum and the gratitude of someone who can finally sleep without wondering
templates.targetNpcOpener: Interesting samples. The mineral composition tells me there's a deposit underneath that's been subjected to seasonal pressure changes. The humming your friend described is the deposit settling, like a bone joint popping. Harmless, but unsettling if you don't know what it is.
templates.targetNpcCloser: Tell them to wait a season and it should quiet down on its own. Nothing to worry about.

templates.expositionText: I've been losing small amounts from my stores every few days, grain and dried goods, a little at a time. The amounts are too regular to be spoilage and too small to be theft. {target_npc} in {target_npc_settlement} noticed something similar last year. Would you ask them what they learned?
templates.acceptText: Good. I'd rather know than keep counting sacks every morning.
templates.declineText: I'll keep counting, then. At least the mystery gives me something to think about.
templates.expositionTurnInText: So {target_npc} says it was {enemy_type_plural} getting in through small gaps and carrying away what they could hold. The amounts would be exactly right for that kind of creature. Now I know what to look for.
templates.conflict1Text: If they've been coming in through gaps, they've got a route established. I need {kill_count} of the {enemy_type_plural} dealt with before they bring in more. Once the path is clear, I can seal the entry points.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: That should break the trail. No new losses this morning, which is the first time in weeks. But I want to replace what they've taken before anyone else notices the shortfall.
templates.conflict2Text: Bring me {gather_count} {quest_item} to top off what's missing. I don't want {settlement_npc} asking why the stores look thinner than they should. The fewer questions, the better.
templates.conflict2TurnInText: Full again. Nobody will know the difference, and the entry points are sealed.
templates.resolutionText: The mystery is solved, the stores are whole, and nothing is getting in anymore. Take {reward_item}. You saved me from weeks more of counting and wondering.

skillCheck.passText: All right, since you're pressing. I was afraid to investigate too hard because I thought it might be a person, one of the families here that's been struggling. I was prepared to let it continue if someone was taking what they needed and too ashamed to ask. Learning it's creatures is actually a relief, because now I can stop feeling guilty about wanting to fix it.
skillCheck.failText: I don't have a theory. That's why I need help.

templates.rewardFlavor: a share of the stores and a few coins from my personal savings
templates.targetNpcOpener: Losing small amounts from the stores? I had the same problem last year. Turned out to be a type of {enemy_type} that moves in family groups, comes in through gaps you wouldn't think big enough, and carries away just enough to avoid being noticed. Very methodical creatures.
templates.targetNpcCloser: Find the entry points, clear the group, and seal the gaps. Problem solves itself after that.

templates.expositionText: Objects around {settlement_name} keep ending up in places they shouldn't be. Fence posts moved ten paces overnight, a water bucket found on a rooftop, tools rearranged in patterns. Bring me a {quest_item} that might help identify what kind of creature has the strength and the temperament for this kind of mischief.
templates.acceptText: Thank you. I've been feeling like I'm losing my grip, and having someone take it seriously helps more than you know.
templates.declineText: Fine. I'll keep finding my tools in new arrangements every morning. At least the mystery keeps life interesting.
templates.expositionTurnInText: Comparing the {quest_item} to the evidence, I can see the marks now. Specific grip patterns, the way things were set down rather than dropped. Whatever's doing this has hands and a sense of humor. Or a sense of territory.
templates.conflict1Text: I want to test something. If I leave out {gather_count} {quest_item} in a particular arrangement, and they get moved the same way, I'll know the pattern is deliberate. Bring them to me and I'll set the trap tonight.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: Every single piece was moved. Same style, same direction. It's definitely the same creature, and now I know its route. The jokes are over.
templates.conflict2Text: The route leads to a cluster of {enemy_type_plural} that have been nesting just out of sight. {kill_count} of them removed should put a stop to the nightly rearrangements. I'd like my fence posts to stay where I put them.
templates.conflict2TurnInText: Everything was exactly where I left it this morning. First time in weeks. The relief is extraordinary.
templates.resolutionText: No more surprises at dawn, no more tools on rooftops. Take {reward_item}. You took something that was making me question my own sanity and turned it into a problem with a solution.

skillCheck.passText: You think the behavior isn't random? The patterns they arrange things in are too consistent to be play, almost like territory marking expressed through objects instead of scent. I've never heard of creatures doing that. The fact that you recognized it as unusual rather than just mischievous tells me there's something deeper here than pest control.
skillCheck.failText: It's strange behavior. I just want it to stop.

templates.rewardFlavor: a fair payment and the satisfaction of my fence posts finally staying put

templates.expositionText: There's been a sound at night near the old foundation, a low scraping that comes and goes. Nobody can find the source, and it's got half the settlement jumping at shadows. Bring me {gather_count} {quest_item} and I'll set up a proper survey of the ground where the sound is loudest.
templates.acceptText: Good. I'd rather have answers than another sleepless night spent listening and wondering.
templates.declineText: I'll keep listening, then. Maybe it'll explain itself eventually.
templates.expositionTurnInText: The survey shows disturbance in the soil, consistent with burrowing. Something's been digging tunnels under the old foundation, and the scraping is the sound of it working through packed earth. Now I just need to deal with what's doing the digging.
templates.conflict1Text: I'm certain it's {enemy_type_plural} tunneling in from the nearby ridge. {kill_count} of them cleared out should collapse their access and stop the noise for good.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: Silence. Actual silence. I stood by the foundation last night and heard nothing but the wind, and it was the most beautiful sound I've ever not heard.
templates.resolutionText: The mystery is solved and the nights are quiet again. Take {reward_item}. You gave {settlement_name} back its peace, and that's worth more than any of us were willing to admit.

skillCheck.passText: All right, I'll tell you the part I've been leaving out. I've known what the sound is for a week, but the last time I raised an alarm about something underground I was wrong and people still remind me of it. I needed someone else to confirm it so they'd actually listen this time. You've given me that.
skillCheck.failText: I genuinely don't know what it is. That's why I need help.

templates.rewardFlavor: the coin I'd set aside for the survey and a quiet night's thanks

templates.expositionText: The dyes I've been using have been losing color within days of setting. Same batch, same technique I've used for years, but the results are washing out like I've never done this before. {target_npc} in {target_npc_settlement} is a {target_npc_role} who works with similar materials. Would you ask them if they've had the same trouble?
templates.acceptText: Good. I need to know if it's my hands or something else before I waste another batch.
templates.declineText: I'll keep experimenting, then. The answer is in the process somewhere, even if I can't find it today.
templates.expositionTurnInText: So {target_npc} says the water source is the issue. A change in mineral content upstream can strip the binding agent right out of the dye. That explains everything, and it means there's a fix.
templates.conflict1Text: I need a {quest_item} to filter the minerals out of the water before I use it. {target_npc} said this particular method works best for the kind of contamination we're dealing with. Bring me one and I'll test it on the next batch.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: The batch held its color overnight, and it's still bright this morning. That's the first time in a month. The problem is solved and I feel like a craftsperson again instead of a fraud.
templates.resolutionText: My work is my work again, and I know why it stopped being. Take {reward_item}. You helped me find an answer I was too close to see, and that's the kind of help that lasts.

skillCheck.passText: The way you described the problem back to me, with real interest, like the craft itself mattered to you. Nobody here talks about dye work like that, they see the finished cloth and shrug. You looked at the process and treated it like it was worth understanding, and that made me feel like an artisan again instead of someone failing at a chore.
skillCheck.failText: It's a craft problem. I just want to fix it.

templates.rewardFlavor: a piece of my best work and honest coin besides
templates.targetNpcOpener: Fading dyes? I had the same problem two seasons back. The water source shifted upstream, picking up minerals that react with the binding agent. The dye sets fine but the color leaches out within days.
templates.targetNpcCloser: Filter the water before use and the problem disappears. I can tell you which method works best.

templates.expositionText: Three months ago, {enemy_type_plural} killed my dog, the best animal I ever had and the only living thing in this house that was glad to see me come home. I've been waiting for someone capable, and now you're here. I want {kill_count} of them dead. That's where this begins.
templates.acceptText: Good. I'll sit here and wait the way I've been waiting, but this time I'll know something is actually happening.
templates.declineText: I didn't expect you to understand. She was a dog. People always say that like it's supposed to make it smaller.
templates.expositionTurnInText: That's {kill_count} fewer of them in the world. I felt something shift in my chest when you told me, and I'm not sure yet if it's relief or if I'm just tired. There's one more piece to this.
templates.conflict1Text: She had a collar I made her myself, and it's still out there somewhere. I know it's just leather and metal, but it's the last thing I made with my own hands that she wore. Bring me a {quest_item} like it, and I'll hang it by the door where she used to sleep.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: That's close enough. I hung it on the hook and stood there for a while. She would have scratched at it, probably. She scratched at everything I made.
templates.resolutionText: The account is closed. I won't pretend it brought her back or made the house less quiet, but it's done, and done matters. Take {reward_item}. {reward_flavor}. Nothing else to do with it now.

skillCheck.passText: You noticed I haven't said her name. Every time I try, my voice does something I'm not ready for anyone to hear. I told the neighbors I was upset about property damage, because a grown adult grieving a dog gets a certain look in this settlement. She was more than that. She was the only friend who never needed me to explain myself.
skillCheck.failText: She was a good dog. There's nothing else to say about it.

templates.rewardFlavor: coin saved for her winter

templates.expositionText: I've been carrying this for a long time. The {enemy_type_plural} that destroyed my orchard took ten years of careful work in a single night. I need a {quest_item} first. Something to mark the boundary of what was mine, so when I settle this, I can see exactly what they took from me.
templates.acceptText: Then we have a deal. I'm not in a hurry, but I'm not patient either. That distinction matters to me.
templates.declineText: Walk away, then. I'll find another. There's always someone willing to do necessary work.
templates.expositionTurnInText: I set it at the edge of where the oldest tree used to stand. Funny how marking what's gone makes you feel it more clearly, not less. Now comes the part I've been holding in my teeth for months.
templates.conflict1Text: I want {kill_count} of those {enemy_type_plural} put down. Not pushed back, not scared off, put down. Every one of them that falls is a year of work acknowledged. I planted those trees as a young person and I watched them grow, and something owes me for what I lost.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: That's the number. I counted them the way I counted the trees. It doesn't balance, because nothing would, but it's as close as the world is going to let me get.
templates.resolutionText: The marker stands and the debt is as paid as it can be. Take {reward_item}, {reward_flavor}. I won't pretend I feel whole, but I feel finished, and that's the next best thing.

skillCheck.passText: You're asking how they got in, and you already know the answer, don't you. The fence line on the north side had been failing for two seasons and I kept patching over the weak spots instead of rebuilding. They didn't break through anything. They walked through a gap I'd been pretending wasn't there. The rage I feel isn't just at them. It's at the version of me that decided it could wait.
skillCheck.failText: They destroyed what I built. The rest is between me and the trees.

templates.rewardFlavor: savings meant for replanting

templates.expositionText: My sister's grave was desecrated by {enemy_type_plural} two weeks ago, the cairn dug through and the stones I'd placed there scattered. I want {kill_count} of them killed. Not for safety, not for prevention. For her.
templates.acceptText: Thank you. I won't ask you to feel what I feel about this. I just need the work done.
templates.declineText: I see. Then I'll rebuild the cairn and keep rebuilding it until they stop, or I stop. Either way.
templates.expositionTurnInText: Good. I rebuilt the stones this morning and they're still standing. But the ones that scattered them aren't the only group in the area, and I want to be thorough.
templates.conflict1Text: There are more of them nearby, the same kind, and I won't rest easy knowing they're within reach of her resting place. {kill_count} more {enemy_type_plural} dealt with, and I can visit her without watching the ground.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: The area is clear. I walked out there just now and it was quiet the way it should be quiet. Like a place that's been left in peace.
templates.resolutionText: She's resting properly again, and nothing is going to disturb that as long as I'm breathing. Take {reward_item}. {reward_flavor}. She would have wanted it given to someone who helped keep her memory whole.

skillCheck.passText: You want to know why I'm this angry? I promised her I would take care of that spot, stood there the day we placed the stones and said it out loud, and I meant it the way you mean a thing you'll never break. When I found the cairn torn apart, I didn't just fail to protect ground. I broke my word to someone who can never hear me make it right.
skillCheck.failText: She was my sister. That's all the reason I need, and all the reason I'll give.

templates.rewardFlavor: coin kept beside her stone

templates.expositionText: The {enemy_type_plural} poisoned our fishing spot last autumn. They fouled the water so thoroughly that the fish haven't come back, and neither has the income I depended on. I need a {quest_item} to test whether the water is recovering. That's the first step.
templates.acceptText: I appreciate that you didn't try to tell me to move on. Moving on isn't what I'm doing here.
templates.declineText: Then I sit by dead water and wait for something to change on its own. I've been good at that.
templates.expositionTurnInText: The water is cleaner than I expected, which means the damage can be undone. But the creatures that caused it are still in the area, and as long as they are, recovery means nothing.
templates.conflict1Text: I want {kill_count} of those {enemy_type_plural} dealt with. Every one of them that stays is a threat to whatever comes back. I'm not watching this get ruined twice.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: Good. The water has a chance now. There's one more thing I need before I can call this settled.
templates.conflict2Text: Bring me a {quest_item}. Something to mark the waterline, so I can track the recovery over the coming weeks. I want to see the numbers change with my own eyes. I've earned that much.
templates.conflict2TurnInText: The marker is set. I'll check it every morning. The first time the line moves in the right direction, I'll know this was worth it.
templates.resolutionText: The water is coming back, slowly, but it's coming. Take {reward_item}: {reward_flavor}. I sat by that bank for months with nothing but anger, and now I have proof that things can get better.

skillCheck.passText: Fine. The part I haven't said is that I was warned this could happen. Someone told me the creatures were moving into the watershed and I dismissed it because I didn't want to spend the season building barriers instead of fishing. My livelihood was destroyed by my own stubbornness, and the anger I feel at those creatures is really anger I can't bear to aim at myself.
skillCheck.failText: They fouled the water. That's the fact, and that's what I'm settling.

templates.rewardFlavor: first catch when fish return

templates.expositionText: A year ago, {enemy_type_plural} came through in the night and took something I can't get back. No witnesses, no story, just the aftermath. I've been stacking that anger carefully, like firewood, and now I'm ready to burn it. {kill_count} of them, start there.
templates.acceptText: Good. I don't need sympathy from you. I need results.
templates.declineText: Then the fire keeps building. I'll find someone else, or I'll go myself and regret the outcome either way.
templates.expositionTurnInText: They're down. I thought I'd feel victorious, and instead I just feel like the room got a little less loud inside my head. There's one more thing.
templates.conflict1Text: Bring me a {quest_item}. I'm going to set it where the thing I lost used to sit, not as a replacement, but as proof that the space doesn't have to stay empty. That's the closest I'll get to peace, and I want to get there.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: I placed it this morning. Stood there looking at it for a long time. It doesn't fill the gap. But it occupies the space, and that's different from empty.
templates.resolutionText: The work is done and the space has something in it again. Take {reward_item} and {reward_flavor}. I'm not going to call it closure, because I don't believe in the word. But the weight shifted, and I can stand a little straighter under it now.

skillCheck.passText: The way you said that, so easily, like grief is something you can just name and set down. I've been trying to do that for a year and couldn't manage the first part. Nobody here talks about loss that directly, they circle it and whisper around it. You just walked up to it and called it what it is, and something in me unclenched.
skillCheck.failText: It's old anger. I don't need it explained to me, just answered.

templates.rewardFlavor: coin from the empty space

templates.expositionText: I had a garden that my mother planted the year I was born. {enemy_type_plural} trampled it flat last spring and I've been quiet about it because nobody here thinks a garden is worth getting angry over. They're wrong. I need a {quest_item} to take a cutting from what survived, before even the roots give up.
templates.acceptText: Thank you. I know it sounds small. It matters to me more than I've let on.
templates.declineText: I'll keep watering the dirt, then. Maybe something will come back on its own. Things do, sometimes.
templates.expositionTurnInText: The cutting took. There's green where there was nothing, and I stared at it for ten minutes before I trusted my own eyes. Now for the part that's been burning in me since spring.
templates.conflict1Text: I want {kill_count} of those {enemy_type_plural} gone. They'll come back otherwise, and they'll do to the new growth what they did to the old. I am not watching that happen twice. Not with her garden.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: They're gone. The ground around the garden is quiet, and the cutting is still standing. I checked it three times today. I'll probably check it three more times tonight.
templates.resolutionText: Something is growing again where my mother's hands put the first seeds in the ground. Take {reward_item}: {reward_flavor}. You helped me save the one thing in this world I can't replace and can't explain the value of to anyone who didn't know her.

skillCheck.passText: You understand what a garden like that means, then. My mother planted it following the old seasonal rites, the way her mother taught her, putting the first seeds in on the morning her tradition marked as the beginning of growth. It was her way of keeping faith with something larger. Watching it get destroyed felt like watching her prayers get answered with silence.
skillCheck.failText: It's a garden. It was my mother's. That's reason enough.

templates.rewardFlavor: savings my mother left me

templates.expositionText: I've always been the person things go wrong for, not dramatically, just reliably. This season the pattern held with thin crops, a leaning shed, and now I can't get enough {quest_item} to keep the household running. I need {gather_count} of it, and I need someone whose luck runs better than mine to go looking.
templates.acceptText: Maybe your luck is changing. Someone showed up who said yes, and that hasn't happened in a while.
templates.declineText: About what I expected. The pattern holds. I'll manage, the way I always manage, which is to say barely.
templates.expositionTurnInText: You actually found enough. I counted it twice because I don't trust good outcomes on the first pass. Now there's the other shoe, and I know it's coming because it always does.
templates.conflict1Text: {enemy_type_plural} have been pressing in closer to {settlement_name}, and naturally they chose my side of the settlement to settle nearest to. {kill_count} of them handled would give me a stretch of ordinary days, which is all I've ever asked for.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: Quiet. Genuinely quiet. I keep waiting for the next thing to go wrong and it hasn't yet. I almost don't know what to do with a day that just works.
templates.resolutionText: I won't say the pattern is broken, I've been disappointed too many times for that. But today was good, and yesterday was good, and I'll take two good days in a row as a sign. Take {reward_item}: {reward_flavor}, and it turns out I didn't need it. You showed me what it looks like when things go right for a change.

skillCheck.passText: It's funny you put it that way, because that's exactly how it feels, like I cause it. Not rationally, but in that part of your mind that keeps count. Everything I touch seems to sour eventually and I've stopped starting new things because I'm afraid I'll ruin them the way I've ruined everything else. Saying it out loud sounds foolish, but there it is.
skillCheck.failText: That's a strange way to ask about someone's luck. I'll manage, just bring me the {quest_item}.

templates.rewardFlavor: what I saved for worse

templates.expositionText: Every time I fix one thing, another breaks. I patched the roof and the well chain snapped. I fixed the chain and {enemy_type_plural} moved in along the road. I need {kill_count} of them dealt with so I can focus on the next disaster without looking over my shoulder at this one.
templates.acceptText: All right. Maybe having someone else take one problem means the chain reaction stops. That's the hope, anyway.
templates.declineText: Then the list grows by one more item. I've gotten very good at lists.
templates.expositionTurnInText: One down. The road is clear, and nothing new broke while you were gone, which I'm counting as a minor miracle. Now for the thing I've been dreading.
templates.conflict1Text: The stores are low because every spare moment has gone to repairs instead of gathering. I need {gather_count} {quest_item} to catch up. It feels like running to stand still, but at least standing still is better than falling behind.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: That puts us even. Not ahead, but even, and even feels luxurious after the month I've had. There's one last piece, and then maybe I can stop bracing for impact.
templates.conflict2Text: I need a {quest_item} to finish the last repair properly. Without it I'll jury-rig something that holds for a week and then we're back to the same cycle. I want to break the pattern for real this time.
templates.conflict2TurnInText: The repair is solid. For the first time in months, everything I look at is in working order. I keep checking, expecting to find something new that's broken, and there's nothing.
templates.resolutionText: Nothing is broken. I've said that three times today and it keeps being true. Take {reward_item}, and {reward_flavor} with it. You helped me step off a wheel I'd been running on so long I forgot what solid ground felt like.

skillCheck.passText: You see the pattern I was hoping nobody would piece together. Each repair failed because I rushed it to get to the next one. The roof leaked because I skipped the sealant, the chain snapped because I reused a worn link, and each shortcut made the next failure come sooner. I built this cycle with my own impatience, and admitting that is harder than any of the repairs.
skillCheck.failText: Things break. I fix them. That's the whole story.

templates.rewardFlavor: the toolbox reckoning coin

templates.expositionText: The season turned early and caught us with half the work undone. Plants that should have had another month are wilting. Stores that should be full are thin. I need {gather_count} {quest_item} to plug the gap before the shortfall becomes a crisis.
templates.acceptText: Thank you. I've been watching the calendar like it owes me something, and it's a relief to have help instead of just time.
templates.declineText: I'll stretch what's left, then. Stretching is what this season is for, apparently.
templates.expositionTurnInText: That'll hold us through the rough patch. The timing still makes no sense to me, but at least we won't go hungry while I puzzle over it. There's one more thing the early turn brought with it.
templates.conflict1Text: The {enemy_type_plural} are moving earlier too, pushed out by the same shift that caught us. {kill_count} of them cleared and we can ride out the rest of this wrong-footed season without adding danger on top of shortage.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: That's better. The pressure from outside has eased, even if the pressure from above hasn't. I can deal with bad weather. Bad weather and bad creatures at the same time was more than I could hold.
templates.resolutionText: We came through a season that had no business being this hard, and we're still standing. Take {reward_item}, and {reward_flavor}. I can't control the weather or the calendar, but I can recognize someone who showed up when both turned against us.

skillCheck.passText: Since you're making me say it plainly. I ignored the early signs because I was too proud to admit the season was turning before I was ready. The old farmers warned me and I waved them off, and now everyone here is paying for my stubbornness. The weather didn't betray us, I did, by not listening when I should have.
skillCheck.failText: Nobody predicted this. It just happened.

templates.rewardFlavor: honest gratitude besides

templates.expositionText: I planted in the same field I've used for twelve years and nothing came up. Not thin growth, not poor yield. Nothing. I need a {quest_item} to test the soil and find out whether the ground has given up on me or whether something else is at work.
templates.acceptText: Thank you. I've been staring at empty rows for three weeks and I needed someone to help me look at them differently.
templates.declineText: Then I keep staring. Maybe the dirt will explain itself eventually.
templates.expositionTurnInText: The test shows the soil has been leached of everything it needs. Something drained it, and not gradually. Now I need to find out what's been doing the draining and stop it before next season is a repeat.
templates.conflict1Text: The {enemy_type_plural} burrowing under the field are the most likely cause. Their nesting behavior disrupts root systems and draws nutrients out of the surrounding soil. {kill_count} of them removed should let the ground start recovering.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: The tunnels are collapsing on their own now that the creatures are gone. I can already see earthworms returning, which is the first sign of recovery. But I still need to feed the soil back to health.
templates.conflict2Text: I need {gather_count} {quest_item} to amend the field. Good compost, the kind of material that puts back what was taken out. It won't fix everything in one season, but it'll give next year's planting a fighting chance.
templates.conflict2TurnInText: The field smells like earth again instead of dust. I'd forgotten what healthy soil looks like, and now I'm looking at it.
templates.resolutionText: Twelve years of good harvests, one bad one, and now the start of recovery. Take {reward_item}, and {reward_flavor}, if the ground keeps its word. I was ready to believe the land had turned against me, and you showed me it was just sick, not hostile. That difference matters more than you know.

skillCheck.passText: The confidence in your voice when you said the field could recover. I needed to hear someone say it like they believed it. My neighbors have been giving me the look people give when they think you should cut your losses, and I was starting to agree with them. But the way you talked about it, like the ground and I still had a future together, that's what kept me from walking away.
skillCheck.failText: It's a field problem. I just want it solved.

templates.rewardFlavor: first harvest when it returns

templates.expositionText: Everything I own is wearing out at the same time. Tools, boots, the hinges on every door, and the {enemy_type_plural} moved in while I was too busy repairing to notice. {kill_count} of them handled, and I can at least face the rest of my problems without an audience.
templates.acceptText: That's one problem with someone else's name on it, and that alone makes the day better.
templates.declineText: Then I add it to the pile. The pile is getting impressive, honestly. Not in a good way.
templates.expositionTurnInText: Better. The outside threat is handled, which means I can turn my attention inward without feeling like I'm ignoring something with teeth. Now for the slow emergency.
templates.conflict1Text: I need {gather_count} {quest_item} to replace the most critical things that have worn through. Not everything, just enough that the house holds together for another season while I catch up on the rest.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: That covers the worst of it. The doors close, the tools work, and I can get through a day without something falling apart in my hands. It a victory, but it's stability, and stability has been in short supply.
templates.resolutionText: I'm holding steady. Not gaining, but not losing ground anymore, and after the month I've had, that feels like a triumph. Take {reward_item}, {reward_flavor}. You helped me stop the bleeding, and that's the thing that had to happen before anything else could.

skillCheck.passText: All right, since you're not going to let me brush it off. I've been telling people I'm handling it because I didn't want anyone to see how far behind I've fallen. The truth is I nearly gave up last week, sat down in the middle of the floor with a broken hinge in my hand and couldn't stand up for an hour. You making me say it is the first time I've admitted out loud that I'm drowning.
skillCheck.failText: Things wear out. It's just happening all at once, which is my particular luck.

templates.rewardFlavor: offered with real gratitude

templates.expositionText: I fixed the irrigation channel last week and it collapsed again yesterday. Same spot, same failure. I need a {quest_item} to reinforce it properly this time, because whatever I've been doing clearly isn't working and I'm tired of repeating myself to the dirt.
templates.acceptText: Good. At least with the right materials I can build something that argues with the ground and wins.
templates.declineText: Then I patch it again with what I have, and I expect to be patching it again next week, and the week after that.
templates.expositionTurnInText: The channel is holding. I walked the full length this morning and found no cracks, no seepage, nothing. That's the first time in three attempts, and I almost don't trust it. But there's more to deal with before I can rest.
templates.conflict1Text: While I was focused on the channel, the supply situation got away from me. I need {gather_count} {quest_item} to get back to where I should have been if the irrigation hadn't eaten all my time and energy.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: Stocked again. I can look at the shelves without doing mental arithmetic about how many days we have left. There's one last thing pressing on me.
templates.conflict2Text: The {enemy_type_plural} have been taking advantage of my distraction. {kill_count} of them put down and I can stop treating every problem as urgent and start treating some of them as solved.
templates.conflict2TurnInText: Solved. Actually solved, not patched, not postponed. The channel holds, the shelves are full, and the road is safe. I keep waiting for the next thing to fail and it hasn't.
templates.resolutionText: For the first time in what feels like forever, I'm not fixing something. I'm just standing here in a place where everything works, and it feels strange and wonderful. Take {reward_item}, and {reward_flavor}. You helped me believe that fixed could actually mean finished.

skillCheck.passText: You noticed something about the failure pattern, didn't you. The collapse keeps happening in the same spot because there's something in the ground there that rejects normal construction. I've dug it up twice and both times there was a residue I couldn't identify, almost crystalline, and warm to the touch. I don't know what it is, but It natural, and pretending it's just bad soil hasn't been working.
skillCheck.failText: The ground is difficult. That's all I know and all I need to know right now.

templates.rewardFlavor: coin I won't need now

templates.expositionText: My cousin and I haven't spoken in two years. It started over a property line and turned into something uglier. {target_npc} in {target_npc_settlement} is a {target_npc_role} who was there when it happened. I need you to go hear their side of it, because I can't bring myself to.
templates.acceptText: Thank you. I've been chewing on this alone too long.
templates.declineText: I figured. Nobody wants to step into someone else's family mess.
templates.expositionTurnInText: So that's how {target_npc} remembers it. Not the same way I do, but close enough that I can't pretend I was completely right. There's one more thing I need before I can face this properly.
templates.conflict1Text: My cousin left a {quest_item} at my place the last time they visited. I've been keeping it out of spite, but I think returning it might open a door. Find me one to replace what I've worn through, so I can give the original back in good condition.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: That'll do. I can make the swap now without it looking like I've been using their things for two years. Which I have, but that's beside the point.
templates.resolutionText: I sent the {quest_item} back with a note, short, nothing grand, just an honest few words. They sent one back this morning. Take {reward_item}. We're talking again, and that's further than I've been in a long time.

skillCheck.passText: Oh, people have told you about the argument already? Then you probably heard a nicer version. The property line wasn't really what started it, I said something about their mother at a gathering, something I knew would cut, and I said it on purpose. The land dispute was just the excuse we both grabbed so we wouldn't have to talk about the real wound.
skillCheck.failText: You're asking a lot of questions about something that's between family. It was about the property line, and that's the version.

templates.rewardFlavor: what I can spare and my word that it meant something
templates.targetNpcOpener: Someone is finally asking about that business? Good. I've been sitting on my account of it for two years and it's been wearing a hole in my patience. Those two are both wrong and both too proud to say it first.
templates.targetNpcCloser: Tell them I said what I said. They can do with it what they want.

templates.expositionText: My brother took half the livestock and moved to the other side of {settlement_name} last spring. Left the fence wide open when he went, and now {enemy_type_plural} come through the gap every other night. I need {kill_count} of them dealt with before I lose what's left.
templates.acceptText: Good. I've been too angry to ask for help until now, and too tired to keep doing this alone.
templates.declineText: Fine. I'll board it up myself and hope for the best. That's what I've been doing anyway.
templates.expositionTurnInText: Quieter already. I walked the perimeter this morning without flinching. But the fence is still a wreck and what he took left holes in more than just the wood.
templates.conflict1Text: I need {gather_count} {quest_item} to patch what's broken. Not just the fence, the whole operation. He didn't just leave, he stripped the place on his way out, and I've been pretending it doesn't matter.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: That's enough to hold things together for the season. I can finally stop counting what's missing and start working with what's here.
templates.resolutionText: The place looks like a place again, instead of a ruin someone walked away from. Take {reward_item}. I'm still angry at him, but at least now I'm angry from a position of having walls that stand up.

skillCheck.passText: You're right, those two things don't add up. He didn't just leave over a disagreement about the farm. I told him our father would have been ashamed of him. I said it in front of his children, and that's why there's a gap in the fence and no conversation about fixing it.
skillCheck.failText: He left. That's all there is to it.

templates.rewardFlavor: the coin I kept hidden from the split and a fair handshake

templates.expositionText: My sister says our grandmother's {quest_item} belongs to her. I say it belongs to me. We've been going back and forth about it for months and neither of us will bend. I need you to find one so I can stop fighting over hers and start using my own.
templates.acceptText: Thank you. I'm exhausted by this and I'd rather solve it than win it.
templates.declineText: I understand. It sounds petty from the outside. It doesn't feel petty from here.
templates.expositionTurnInText: Good. Now I have my own and I don't need hers. But I owe her a conversation I've been avoiding.
templates.conflict1Text: Go find {target_npc} in {target_npc_settlement}. They're a {target_npc_role} and a mutual friend. Tell them I'm ready to talk if she is. I'd rather hear it through someone we both trust than show up at her door unannounced.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: {target_npc} thinks she'll agree to meet? That's more than I expected. I've been bracing for a longer silence.
templates.resolutionText: We sat down yesterday. Didn't yell, didn't accuse, just talked like two people who remember being children together. Take {reward_item}. You gave me a way to stop losing my sister over a thing that should never have mattered this much.

skillCheck.passText: Fine, I'll say it plainly. She's not wrong about the claim. Our grandmother told her she could have it, and I know that because I was standing in the doorway when she said it. I took it anyway after the funeral because I was grieving and I wanted something to hold.
skillCheck.failText: The claim is complicated. I'd rather not go into the details.

templates.rewardFlavor: a few coins and something I baked this morning
templates.targetNpcOpener: I've been waiting for one of them to crack first. Honestly, I thought it would be her. Tell the other one I'll pass the message along, but they'd better mean it this time.
templates.targetNpcCloser: I'll send word by tomorrow. No promises on tone, but the door will be open.

templates.expositionText: My uncle and I have been arguing about who maintains the shared storehouse for {settlement_name}. Neither of us will do it while the other claims authority over it, so the whole thing is falling apart. I need {gather_count} {quest_item} to fix it myself before it collapses and makes us both look like fools.
templates.acceptText: Appreciated. At least someone around here is willing to do practical work instead of arguing about who has the right to.
templates.declineText: Then the storehouse keeps rotting and we keep fighting. Wonderful.
templates.expositionTurnInText: That's enough to shore it up. The storehouse won't fall down this week, at least. But there's still the matter of what's been getting in while the walls were weak.
templates.conflict1Text: {enemy_type_plural} have been nesting in the gaps. {kill_count} of them cleared out and we can actually use the building again without holding our breath every time we open the door.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: Clean and quiet. My uncle walked past this morning and didn't say a word, which from him is practically an apology.
templates.resolutionText: The storehouse is standing, the vermin are gone, and my uncle left a sack of grain by my door this morning without a note. Take {reward_item}. I think we're done fighting about it. Neither of us will say so, but we both know.

skillCheck.passText: Look, the real problem is that we're the last two members of this family in {settlement_name}, and neither of us knows how to say that out loud. The storehouse is just the thing we fight about so we don't have to talk about how alone we both are. He knows it and I know it.
skillCheck.failText: It's a disagreement about responsibility. That's all it is.

templates.rewardFlavor: a fair share from the stores and an honest thanks

templates.expositionText: My nephew and I don't see eye to eye on much these days. He moved to {target_npc_settlement} and I'm told he's been saying things about me that aren't entirely fair. I need someone to go talk to {target_npc}, a {target_npc_role} who knows us both, and get the honest version.
templates.acceptText: Thank you. I need to know what I'm dealing with before I decide how to feel about it.
templates.declineText: Can't blame you. Family business makes everyone uncomfortable.
templates.expositionTurnInText: So {target_npc} confirmed it. He's been telling people I cheated him out of his share. That stings, but at least now I know what I'm answering to.
templates.conflict1Text: There's a {quest_item} his father left for him that I've been holding in trust. I should have sent it ages ago. Find me one in decent shape so I can include something of my own alongside it, as a gesture.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: Good. I'll send both together with a letter. Whether he reads it is up to him.
templates.conflict2Text: While all this has been churning, {enemy_type_plural} have been taking advantage of my distraction. {kill_count} of them cleared and I can focus on the family problem without worrying about the practical one.
templates.conflict2TurnInText: That's handled. I can think straight again without listening for trouble at the fence line.
templates.resolutionText: He wrote back, one line, but a real one. We'll see where it goes. Take {reward_item}. You helped me stop a feud from becoming permanent, and that's worth more than I can properly say.

skillCheck.passText: You have a way of putting people at ease, you know that? All right. The truth is I was hard on him when he was younger. I raised him the way my father raised me, which I've come to understand was not gently.
skillCheck.failText: He's young. Young people hold grudges differently.

templates.rewardFlavor: what I've set aside and a debt I'll remember
templates.targetNpcOpener: You're asking about those two? I've known them both since before the boy could walk. The uncle means well, but he's got a heavy hand with advice, and the nephew has his mother's temper. They're more alike than either of them will ever admit.
templates.targetNpcCloser: Tell the uncle what I told you. All of it. He can decide what to do with the truth.

templates.expositionText: My sister-in-law blames me for her husband leaving {settlement_name}. She's not entirely wrong, but the way she tells it, I'm the villain of the whole affair. Meanwhile the {enemy_type_plural} out past the orchards don't care about our drama, and I need {kill_count} of them gone before they ruin what's left of the harvest.
templates.acceptText: Thank you. Hard to tend a feud and a farm at the same time, and the farm is the one that feeds people.
templates.declineText: I'll manage. The {enemy_type_plural} aren't as stubborn as she is, at least.
templates.expositionTurnInText: The orchard side is quiet now. That's one problem I can actually solve with effort. The other one takes a different kind of work.
templates.conflict1Text: The fence rows need mending and I'm short on materials. {gather_count} {quest_item} would let me patch them up. I've been so caught up in the argument that I let the land go, and that's not right.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: That's better. The place looks cared for again. Maybe if she sees me putting the work in, she'll reconsider who the real problem is.
templates.conflict2Text: I need you to find {target_npc} in {target_npc_settlement}. They're a {target_npc_role} and a friend of her husband's. If anyone can tell me whether reconciliation is possible or whether I should stop trying, it's them.
templates.conflict2TurnInText: {target_npc} said she's tired of being angry too? That's more hope than I've had in months.
templates.resolutionText: I don't think we'll ever be close. But she dropped off a jar of preserves yesterday without a word, and I left a bundle of firewood by her gate this morning. Take {reward_item}. That's the language we speak now, and it's enough.

skillCheck.passText: You noticed the charm on the gate, didn't you. She hung it there the week he left. It's a warding sign, old tradition. Means she considers my home a source of harm to her family.
skillCheck.failText: We have our disagreements. That's the polite way to describe it.

templates.rewardFlavor: a share of the harvest and honest gratitude
templates.targetNpcOpener: I wondered when someone would come asking about those two. Her husband told me the whole story before he left, and the truth is messier than either side admits. She's hurt, and the one you're asking for is guilty, but not as guilty as she needs to believe.
templates.targetNpcCloser: Tell them both to stop performing for an audience and just talk. I'll say the same to her if she asks.

templates.expositionText: {settlement_npc} hasn't been themselves for weeks now. Talking to walls, forgetting names, staring at the treeline like they're waiting for something to come out of it. {target_npc} in {target_npc_settlement} is a {target_npc_role} who might know what's happening. Please, go ask them.
templates.acceptText: Thank you. I didn't know who else to turn to.
templates.declineText: I see. I'll keep watching them and hoping it passes on its own.
templates.expositionTurnInText: So {target_npc} has seen this before? That's something. At least it has a name, even if the name is frightening. They mentioned something that might help.
templates.conflict1Text: {target_npc} said a {quest_item} could ease the worst of it. I don't have one and I've looked everywhere nearby. If you can find one, I'll do whatever it takes to get it to {settlement_npc} in time.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: You found one. I could weep. Their eyes were clearer this morning after I used it. They called me by name for the first time in a week.
templates.resolutionText: They're not cured, but they're present again. They asked about the weather and meant it. Take {reward_item}. You gave me back a person I was watching disappear, and I don't have words large enough for that.

skillCheck.passText: You're asking the right question. Why would it come on like this, all at once, if there's no history of it. It runs in their family. Their mother went the same way when I was young enough to watch, and I've been dreading this for years, telling myself the signs meant something else every time one appeared.
skillCheck.failText: It came on suddenly. That's all I know for certain.

templates.rewardFlavor: everything I can spare and a gratitude that won't fade
templates.targetNpcOpener: Someone from {settlement_name} is asking about confusion and memory loss? I've seen it before, in the old days. It's an illness, and there are things that help if you catch it early enough.
templates.targetNpcCloser: Don't wait on this. Time matters more than they think.

templates.expositionText: I found {settlement_npc} standing in the rain last night, barefoot, talking to someone who wasn't there. They've been getting worse for days. I've heard a {quest_item} can bring clarity, even briefly. I need one before they wander off and don't come back.
templates.acceptText: Hurry, please. I left them sleeping but I don't know how long that will last.
templates.declineText: Then I'll keep watch myself and pray they don't slip out again.
templates.expositionTurnInText: They drank it down without a fight, like some part of them knew they needed it. The fog lifted a little. They looked at me and said my name, twice, like they were checking it still fit. But I need more help than a single remedy can give.
templates.conflict1Text: Find {target_npc} in {target_npc_settlement}. They're a {target_npc_role} and they knew {settlement_npc} before all this started. I need someone who remembers who they used to be. Maybe hearing a familiar voice will anchor them.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: {target_npc} is willing to come? I could have collapsed with relief when you told me. Someone who knows them, who can reach the part of them that's still in there.
templates.resolutionText: {settlement_npc} sat with {target_npc} for an hour this afternoon and laughed twice. I'd forgotten what their laugh sounded like. Take {reward_item}. The road ahead is still long, but the person walking it is someone I recognize again.

skillCheck.passText: That was a gentle way to ask, and I'm grateful for it. I've been telling people they're just tired, just overworked. The truth is I saw them try to walk into the fire three nights ago, and I barely caught them. I haven't slept since.
skillCheck.failText: They've been unwell. I'm handling it the best I can.

templates.rewardFlavor: what little I've kept back and a promise I'll repay you properly when things settle
templates.targetNpcOpener: I remember them well. Sharpest mind in {settlement_name} when I knew them. If they're struggling the way you describe, then something has gone deeply wrong, and I'd like to help if I can.
templates.targetNpcCloser: I'll come as soon as I can arrange things here. Tell them I'm on my way.

templates.expositionText: My partner has been drawing the same symbol on every surface they can reach, walls, tables, the dirt outside. They can't explain it and they get agitated when I try to cover them. {target_npc} in {target_npc_settlement} is a {target_npc_role} who studies old patterns. Go ask them what it means.
templates.acceptText: Please be quick. They've started scratching them into the furniture and I'm running out of surfaces to hide.
templates.declineText: I'll scrub the walls again and pretend tomorrow will be different.
templates.expositionTurnInText: So {target_npc} recognized the pattern? That's terrifying and comforting at the same time. At least it means something, even if the meaning is frightening. They told you about a remedy.
templates.conflict1Text: I need a {quest_item}. {target_npc} said it would quiet the compulsion, or at least slow it down. Find me one and I'll try anything at this point.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: They stopped drawing this morning. Sat at the table and ate breakfast like a normal person. Then they looked at the marks on the walls and asked me who made them. They didn't remember.
templates.resolutionText: The walls are clean now and so is their mind, or close enough. They still pause sometimes and stare at their hands, but then they shake it off and carry on. Take {reward_item}. I was watching them lose themselves one symbol at a time, and you stopped it.

skillCheck.passText: All right, since you're not going to let me dodge this. I tried to stop them last week by grabbing their hands. They looked at me like I was the stranger, not them. Like I was the one who didn't belong in this house.
skillCheck.failText: It's been difficult. I'd rather focus on solutions than details.

templates.rewardFlavor: what coin we have between us and something warm for the road
templates.targetNpcOpener: Show me the pattern they've been drawing. Yes, I've seen this before, in old texts. It random and It meaningless. The mind is reaching for something it can't quite name, and the hand follows.
templates.targetNpcCloser: Act quickly. The longer it goes, the harder it is to untangle.

templates.expositionText: My mother goes in and out. Some hours she's herself, sharp and warm and present, and other hours she doesn't know where she is or who I am. I need a {quest_item} that used to help her in the early days. The episodes are getting longer and I'm running out of the good hours.
templates.acceptText: Thank you. I keep thinking if I can just get through the next bad stretch, the good one will last longer. I know that's not how it works, but it's what I have.
templates.declineText: I'll find one myself when she sleeps. The trick is getting back before she wakes up confused.
templates.expositionTurnInText: She took it without question. Sat still for a minute, then looked at me and asked about my day like nothing had happened. I need to follow up with someone who understands this.
templates.conflict1Text: {target_npc} in {target_npc_settlement} is a {target_npc_role} who's dealt with this kind of thing before. I need them to know what's happening so they can tell me what to expect. The not knowing is almost worse than the episodes themselves.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: {target_npc} said it can be managed? That word, managed, is the most beautiful thing I've heard in weeks. Not cured, I know. But managed means she stays.
templates.resolutionText: She had four good days in a row. She made soup and told me I looked tired, which means she's well enough to worry about someone other than herself. Take {reward_item}. You didn't fix everything, but you gave me a way to keep going, and that's what I needed most.

skillCheck.passText: Fine. You're direct, so I'll be direct. Last week she didn't recognize me for an entire day. Twenty hours of being a stranger in my own mother's house.
skillCheck.failText: The episodes come and go. I'm coping.

templates.rewardFlavor: what she'd want me to give someone who helped and a little extra from me
templates.targetNpcOpener: I've treated conditions like this before. The pattern you're describing is consistent with something treatable, though not quickly. There are things the family can do daily that make a real difference over time.
templates.targetNpcCloser: I'll write down everything I know. Take it back and follow it closely.

templates.expositionText: My husband has been sleepwalking, but it's worse than that. He walks to the same place every night, stands there with his eyes open, and speaks to people who aren't there. {target_npc} in {target_npc_settlement} is a {target_npc_role} who might understand this. Please, go before he walks somewhere I can't follow.
templates.acceptText: Thank you. I've been tying his wrist to mine at night, but I'm so tired I'm afraid I'll sleep through it.
templates.declineText: I'll keep tying the knot tighter, then. It's all I know to do.
templates.expositionTurnInText: So {target_npc} has encountered this? That steadies me more than you know. They said there's something that might help.
templates.conflict1Text: I need a {quest_item}. {target_npc} described what to do with it, and I wrote every word down. If this works, he stays in bed and I stay sane.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: He slept through the night. Both of us did. I woke up with his wrist still tied to mine and he was just sleeping, normally, like any other person. I don't think I'll untie the knot for a while yet, but at least now I'm not afraid to close my eyes.
templates.resolutionText: Three nights running and he hasn't walked once. He's even starting to remember the dreams, which {target_npc} said is a good sign. Take {reward_item}. You pulled us back from something I couldn't name and couldn't fight alone.

skillCheck.passText: You have a calm about you that makes hard things easier to say. He talks to his dead sister during the walks. I recognized her name the second night. She died when they were children, and he's never once mentioned her while awake.
skillCheck.failText: He walks and he talks. I just want it to stop.

templates.rewardFlavor: what we've saved between us and a meal whenever you visit
templates.targetNpcOpener: Night walking with coherent speech? That's uncommon, but I've read about it. The mind is processing something it can't handle during waking hours, so it borrows the body while it's unguarded. There are remedies, but the real work is understanding what's driving it.
templates.targetNpcCloser: I'll prepare instructions. Follow them carefully and come back to me if the pattern changes.

templates.expositionText: My son came back from a trip to {other_settlement} and he hasn't been right since. He flinches at sounds that aren't there and keeps checking the locks. I've heard a {quest_item} can settle the nerves when they're this frayed. I need one before he stops leaving his room entirely.
templates.acceptText: Please hurry. He trusts me less every day, and I'm terrified of the day he stops trusting me altogether.
templates.declineText: I'll keep sitting outside his door, then. At least he knows someone is there.
templates.expositionTurnInText: He drank it without fighting me on it. I think part of him knows he needs help, even if the rest of him won't say so. His hands stopped shaking by evening, but I need to understand what happened to him.
templates.conflict1Text: {target_npc} in {target_npc_settlement} is a {target_npc_role} who was on the same road around the same time. They might know what he saw. I can't ask him directly because every time I try, he shuts down completely.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: So {target_npc} saw something out there too, and they're managing it better because they had someone to talk to. That confirms it's real, not something his mind invented. Knowing that changes everything.
templates.resolutionText: He opened his curtains this morning, and then he opened the door. He still checks the locks, but only twice now instead of ten times. Take {reward_item}. He's finding his way back, and I have you to thank for the first steps.

skillCheck.passText: You understand this kind of thing, don't you. The old traditions talk about the soul being shaken loose by what it witnesses. I grew up hearing those stories, and I dismissed them until I watched my own son come home with something missing from behind his eyes. Now I understand what the elders were describing.
skillCheck.failText: Something scared him badly. That's all I've been able to piece together.

templates.rewardFlavor: what a worried parent can scrape together and lasting thanks
templates.targetNpcOpener: The road from {other_settlement}? Yes, I was on it about the same time. I saw something out there that I'm still working through myself. If your boy saw the same thing, I'm not surprised he's struggling.
templates.targetNpcCloser: Tell his family he's not imagining it. That alone will help more than they realize.

templates.expositionText: I've been maintaining the irrigation ditches for {settlement_name} since before most people here arrived. Nobody asked me to start and nobody pays me to continue. The channels need reinforcing and I'm short {gather_count} {quest_item}. A hand with that would go a long way.
templates.acceptText: Appreciated. The water runs whether anyone notices or not, and that's the way I prefer it.
templates.declineText: Fair enough. I'll get to it in my own time. The ditches won't dig themselves, but they'll wait.
templates.expositionTurnInText: That's solid material. The channels will hold for another season now. There's one more thing that's been nagging at me.
templates.conflict1Text: A {target_npc_role} named {target_npc} over in {target_npc_settlement} designed the original layout years ago. I've been making adjustments on instinct, but I'd like their opinion on whether I'm doing it right. Would you ask them?
templates.conflict1TurnInText: {target_npc} said my changes were sound? I'll admit that matters to me more than it should. It's good to know the work holds up under someone else's eye.
templates.resolutionText: The water runs clean and the channels are strong. Nobody will notice, and that's the mark of the job done right. Take {reward_item}. The work continues regardless, but it's lighter knowing someone saw it.

skillCheck.passText: You caught the part that doesn't add up. Nobody maintains ditches for free without a reason. My daughter died in a flood three years ago, upstream, because the channels there weren't maintained. That's the piece I leave out when I tell the story, because saying it out loud means admitting I couldn't save her by digging faster.
skillCheck.failText: Somebody has to tend them. I've got the time and the back for it.

templates.rewardFlavor: a day's honest wage and a drink from the cleanest water in the region
templates.targetNpcOpener: Someone is still maintaining those channels? I designed them to last ten years, not twenty. Whoever's been adapting them has a good sense of how water moves. The adjustments are better than what I'd have suggested myself.
templates.targetNpcCloser: Tell them to keep the western fork clear through autumn. That's the only risk I can see from here.

templates.expositionText: I walk the perimeter of {settlement_name} every evening. Not for pay, not because anyone asked, just because I believe someone should. The {enemy_type_plural} along the south ridge have been thickening, and I can't thin them alone anymore. I need {kill_count} of them dealt with.
templates.acceptText: Good. I'll use the breathing room to reinforce the watch points.
templates.declineText: I'll keep at it. One pair of eyes is better than none.
templates.expositionTurnInText: The ridge is quieter already. I walked it last night and the only sound was the wind, which is exactly how it should be. There's one more thing I've been putting off.
templates.conflict1Text: I've been needing a {quest_item} to replace the one that finally gave out on me. Hard to keep a nightly routine going with broken equipment. If you spot one, I'd be grateful.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: That's well made. It'll see me through another year of evenings, at least.
templates.resolutionText: The perimeter is secure, my gear is sound, and {settlement_name} sleeps a little safer because of it. Take {reward_item}. I set it aside for this kind of help, and I'm glad it went to someone who understood the purpose.

skillCheck.passText: You saw through the practical explanation, didn't you. The truth is my wife left me two years ago and the house is too quiet at night. Walking the perimeter gives me a reason to be outside and a reason to be tired enough to sleep when I get back. The fact that it helps people is real, but it isn't the whole reason.
skillCheck.failText: I believe in the work. That's enough to keep me walking.

templates.rewardFlavor: what I've set aside from my own stores and a firm handshake

templates.expositionText: I've been leaving food at the doors of families in {settlement_name} who are too proud to ask for help. Nobody knows it's me and I'd like to keep it that way. I need a {quest_item} to keep the operation running smoothly. The larder is getting thin.
templates.acceptText: Thank you. The families don't know and the families don't need to know. That's the whole point.
templates.declineText: I'll stretch what I have. There's always a way to make less feel like enough.
templates.expositionTurnInText: Perfect. That'll keep the system running for another few weeks. But I need to restock the reserves or I'll be the one going hungry.
templates.conflict1Text: I need {gather_count} {quest_item} to fill out the stores. I've been eating less myself to keep up the supply, and I'd rather not do that through another winter.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: That's a proper stock. I can eat my own meals again without doing the math on who needs it more. That's a relief I didn't know I was waiting for.
templates.resolutionText: The doors will keep getting their packages and nobody will know who put them there. That's exactly how it should work. Take {reward_item}. You helped me help people who would refuse it if they knew where it came from, and that's the cleanest kind of good there is.

skillCheck.passText: You're pushing on this because you can see I'm thinner than I should be. All right. I've been giving away more than I can afford because the alternative is watching children go to bed hungry and doing nothing. I know the math doesn't work.
skillCheck.failText: The stores run a little lean sometimes. It's nothing unusual.

templates.rewardFlavor: a fair wage and my quiet thanks

templates.expositionText: I've been rebuilding the old bridge north of {settlement_name} on my own. It's been closed for two years and people just go around, but going around adds half a day to every trip. I need {gather_count} {quest_item} to finish the span.
templates.acceptText: Good. The bridge doesn't care who fixes it, only that someone does.
templates.declineText: I'll get there. It's taken me two years already, so another few months won't change the outcome.
templates.expositionTurnInText: The span is nearly complete. I can see the other side for the first time since I started. But there's a complication.
templates.conflict1Text: {enemy_type_plural} have been denning in the old abutments on the far side. {kill_count} of them cleared out and I can finish the decking without worrying about what's underneath it.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: Clean. I can work without looking over my shoulder now. One last thing before I call it done.
templates.conflict2Text: {target_npc} in {target_npc_settlement} is a {target_npc_role} who built the original bridge. I'd like them to know someone finished what time undid. Would you carry word?
templates.conflict2TurnInText: {target_npc} was pleased? Good. I didn't do it for the recognition, but knowing the original builder approves means the work is sound.
templates.resolutionText: The bridge is open, and I walked across it this morning without a creak. People will use it and never think about who rebuilt it, and that's fine. Take {reward_item}. You deserve something tangible for the work.

skillCheck.passText: You're wondering why someone would rebuild a bridge nobody asked for. My brother drowned in the river below it, trying to ford the crossing after the old bridge collapsed. If it had been standing, he'd have walked across and come home for dinner. That's the piece I leave out when I tell the story.
skillCheck.failText: It needs fixing. That's reason enough for me.

templates.rewardFlavor: what I've saved from honest work and a clear conscience
templates.targetNpcOpener: Someone rebuilt my bridge? After all these years? I thought the river would swallow what was left of it before anyone bothered. That's a stubborn person doing stubborn work, and I mean that as the highest compliment I know.
templates.targetNpcCloser: Tell them the joint pattern I used was meant to flex in spring floods. If they matched it, the bridge will outlive us both.

templates.expositionText: I've been replanting the orchard east of {settlement_name} for three years. The old one died off in a blight and nobody else stepped up. The {enemy_type_plural} keep digging up the saplings at night, and I need {kill_count} of them gone before I lose another season's work.
templates.acceptText: Good. Every sapling they destroy is a year of patience lost. I could use a break from patience.
templates.declineText: I'll replant what they dig up. Again. It's what I've been doing.
templates.expositionTurnInText: The saplings are standing this morning, all of them. That's the first time I've been able to say that in weeks. Now I can focus on the next planting.
templates.conflict1Text: I need {gather_count} {quest_item} to prepare the soil for the next row. The ground on the east slope is stubborn, and it needs amending before anything will take root there.
templates.conflict1TurnInText: The soil is ready. By next season there will be fruit on these branches, and the people who eat it won't know my name. That's the whole idea.
templates.resolutionText: The orchard is taking shape. In five years it will feed families who never met me, and in ten it will look like it's always been there. Take {reward_item}. The trees are the real payment, but you deserve something you can hold today.

skillCheck.passText: I like how you carry yourself. Open, genuine. It makes me want to be honest in return. I don't have children.
skillCheck.failText: It's good land going to waste. Someone should use it.

templates.rewardFlavor: a basket from last year's surviving trees and a fair handful of coin
